meta.tagline: Nombrar es crear — To name is to create

youtube.title: Your First Words in Spanish — Hola, Yo Soy...
youtube.description: Learn your very first Spanish words with Yaguara, the jaguar spirit guide. In El Viaje del Jaguar, every word you learn lights up the world. Start with 'Hola,' 'yo soy,' and 'tu eres' — the building blocks of identity in Spanish. From the Colombian rainforest to your screen, completely free at babelfree.com.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Close-up of golden jaguar eyes in shadow. Text fades in: 'Hola.'
[5-15s] HOST: 'In Colombia, there's a belief that when you name something, it comes alive. Today, you're going to learn the most powerful words in Spanish — the ones that tell the world who you are.'
[15-25s] On screen: 'Yo soy ___' (I am ___). HOST: 'Yo soy means I am. Yo — that's me. Soy — I am. Yo soy Ana. Yo soy Marco. Now you — yo soy... and your name.'
[25-40s] On screen: 'Tu eres ___' (You are ___). HOST: 'Now flip it. Tu eres — you are. Tu eres viajero — you are a traveler. That's what Yaguara, the jaguar spirit guide, calls you when you start your journey on Babelfree.'
[40-55s] Quick montage showing game interface: Yaguara saying 'Yo soy Yaguara. Y tu eres... viajero.' HOST: 'In the game, when you say your name for the first time, it glows in the air like a firefly. That's not just poetic — that's how learning should feel.'
[55-60s] End card: babelfree.com — 'Nombrar es crear.'

youtube.title: 6 Animals in Spanish You'll Never Forget
youtube.description: Learn jaguar, pajaro, mono, rana, pez, and more — the animals of the Colombian rainforest. Each word comes from the actual game content of El Viaje del Jaguar, where naming things makes them glow. Free at babelfree.com.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Lush rainforest sounds. Text: '6 animals. 6 names. Ready?'
[5-12s] HOST: 'In El Viaje del Jaguar, things only exist when you name them. So let's bring 6 animals to life.'
[12-20s] Image: jaguar. 'El jaguar — the jaguar. Say it: ha-goo-AR. In our game, this is Yaguara, your guide.'
[20-28s] Image: bird. 'El pajaro — the bird. Pah-ha-ro. Listen for the stress on the first A.'
[28-35s] Image: monkey. 'El mono — the monkey. Easy one. Mo-no. Two syllables.'
[35-42s] Image: frog. 'La rana — the frog. Rah-na. Notice it's LA rana, not EL. Feminine.'
[42-48s] Image: fish. 'El pez — the fish. Just three letters. Peth (in Spain) or Pes (in Colombia).'
[48-55s] Image: forest. 'Bonus — la selva — the jungle, the rainforest. Sel-va. Where all these animals live.'
[55-60s] HOST: 'All 6 of these words come from Destination 2 of El Viaje del Jaguar — completely free at babelfree.com. Nombrar es crear — to name is to create.'

youtube.title: Spanish Colors Through Colombian Landscapes
youtube.description: Learn verde, rojo, azul, blanco, negro, and amarillo through the breathtaking ecosystems of Colombia. From the green of the selva to the blue of the Caribbean coast. Free Spanish lessons at babelfree.com.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Split screen: 6 Colombian landscapes, each a different dominant color. Text: 'Colombia teaches you colors.'
[5-15s] HOST: 'Colombia has 8 ecosystems — bosque, costa, desierto, islas, llanos, nevada, selva, sierra. Each one is a different color. Let's learn them.'
[15-22s] Rainforest shot. 'Verde — green. Ver-de. La selva es verde — the jungle is green.'
[22-29s] Sunset shot. 'Rojo — red. Ro-ho. Remember, the J in Spanish sounds like H. La flor es roja — the flower is red.'
[29-36s] Ocean shot. 'Azul — blue. Ah-ZOOL. El cielo es azul — the sky is blue.'
[36-42s] Snow-capped mountain. 'Blanco — white. BLAN-co. La nieve es blanca — the snow is white.'
[42-48s] Night sky. 'Negro — black. NEH-gro. La noche es negra — the night is black.'
[48-54s] Sunflower field. 'Amarillo — yellow. Ah-mah-REE-yo. El sol es amarillo — the sun is yellow.'
[54-60s] HOST: 'In Destination 3, Yaguara asks you: Como es el mundo? — What is the world like? Now you can describe it. babelfree.com'

youtube.title: Count to 10 in Spanish — With Moons and Magic
youtube.description: Learn numbers 1-10 in Spanish the way Yaguara teaches them: counting moons reflected in the river at night. Uno, dos, tres... all the way to diez. From the game El Viaje del Jaguar, free at babelfree.com.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Night sky reflected in water. Moons appear one by one. Text: 'Uno...'
[5-12s] HOST: 'In the game, Yaguara counts moons on the river. Let's count with her.'
[12-18s] 'Uno — one. Dos — two. Tres — three.' Each number appears with a glowing moon.
[18-25s] 'Cuatro — four. Cinco — five.' HOST: 'Yaguara says: Tengo cinco anos — I am five years old. Tengo means I have. In Spanish, you HAVE years, you don't ARE years old.'
[25-32s] 'Seis — six. Siete — seven.' HOST: 'Siete dias — seven days. Una semana — one week.'
[32-40s] 'Ocho — eight. Nueve — nine. Diez — ten.' HOST: 'Diez lunas — ten moons. That's how Yaguara counts time.'
[40-50s] HOST: 'Now the days of the week — they come from Destination 4 too: lunes, martes, miercoles, jueves, viernes, sabado, domingo. Seven words, one week.'
[50-60s] HOST: 'Cuantos anos tienes? — How old are you? Practice at babelfree.com, completely free. The journey has 89 destinations and 18,152 games.'

youtube.title: How to Say 'I Like' in Spanish — Me Gusta Explained
youtube.description: The most useful phrase in Spanish, explained through a chaotic baby jaguar named Rio who likes EVERYTHING. Me gusta, no me gusta, te gusta — master them all. Free at babelfree.com.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Text on screen: 'ME GUSTA TODO!' with bouncy animation. Splash sound.
[5-15s] HOST: 'There's a character in El Viaje del Jaguar named Rio. He's a baby jaguar who bursts out of the river screaming ME GUSTA TODO — I like everything. Let's learn from his enthusiasm.'
[15-25s] On screen: 'Me gusta + noun.' HOST: 'Me gusta el agua — I like water. Me gusta el sol — I like the sun. Me gusta el pescado — I like fish. The thing you like comes AFTER me gusta.'
[25-35s] On screen: 'No me gusta.' HOST: 'Yaguara says: No me gusta el silencio — I don't like silence. Just add NO at the beginning.'
[35-45s] On screen: 'Te gusta?' HOST: 'To ask someone: Que te gusta a ti? — What do you like? A ti means to you. Yaguara asks this question at the end of Destination 5.'
[45-55s] On screen: 'Me gustan + plural.' HOST: 'When you like multiple things: Me gustan los sonidos del bosque — I like the sounds of the forest. GusTAN, not gusTAH. The verb matches what you like, not you.'
[55-60s] HOST: 'Be like Rio. Like everything. Learn everything. babelfree.com — it's free.'

youtube.title: A Day in Spanish — Yo Despierto, Camino, Bebo...
youtube.description: Learn how to describe your daily routine in Spanish using present tense -AR verbs: caminar, nadar, hablar, escuchar, comer, beber. Follow Yaguara through a day in the Colombian rainforest. Free at babelfree.com.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Sunrise over jungle canopy. Text: 'Un dia en el mundo.'
[5-15s] HOST: 'Destination 6 is called Un dia en el mundo — A day in the world. Yaguara describes her day, and by the end, you can describe yours.'
[15-25s] Sunrise. HOST: 'Yo despierto con el sol — I wake up with the sun. Despierto — I wake up. Camino al rio — I walk to the river. Camino — I walk.'
[25-35s] River scene. HOST: 'Bebo agua — I drink water. Bebo. Then: Como fruta — I eat fruit. Como. Notice — these verbs end in O when it's yo — I.'
[35-45s] Forest sounds. HOST: 'Escucho los sonidos — I listen to the sounds. Escucho. Hablo con el tucan — I talk with the toucan. Hablo. Descanso en un arbol — I rest in a tree. Descanso.'
[45-55s] HOST: 'Six verbs, one pattern: caminar becomes camino, hablar becomes hablo, escuchar becomes escucho. Drop the -AR, add -O. That's present tense for yo.'
[55-60s] HOST: 'Que haces tu todos los dias? — What do you do every day? Start your journey at babelfree.com'

youtube.title: El and La — Why Spanish Has Two 'The's
youtube.description: Master Spanish articles el and la with real examples from the game: el rio, la selva, el arbol, la rana, el pajaro, la flor. Understand masculine and feminine nouns through the naming ritual of El Viaje del Jaguar.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Two glowing words float: 'EL' and 'LA.' Text: 'The biggest confusion in Spanish, solved.'
[5-15s] HOST: 'English has one word for THE. Spanish has two: EL for masculine nouns and LA for feminine. In the game, when Yaguara names the world, she teaches you both.'
[15-25s] HOST: 'EL: el rio — the river. El arbol — the tree. El pajaro — the bird. El pez — the fish. El jaguar. El sol — the sun.'
[25-35s] HOST: 'LA: la selva — the jungle. La rana — the frog. La flor — the flower. La luna — the moon. La comida — the food. La fruta — the fruit.'
[35-45s] HOST: 'Pattern? Most words ending in O are EL. Most ending in A are LA. But watch out — el agua, el dia — some A-words are masculine. You'll learn these naturally in the game.'
[45-55s] HOST: 'In Destination 2, Yaguara says: Cada cosa tiene un nombre — every thing has a name. Y cada nombre tiene una luz — and every name has a light. That's how you learn articles — by naming things until they glow.'
[55-60s] End card: 'Nombrar es crear. babelfree.com'

youtube.title: Ser vs Estar — The Most Colombian Explanation Ever
youtube.description: Learn the difference between SER and ESTAR through the Colombian rainforest: El rio ES frio (the river IS cold, always) vs Yo ESTOY aqui (I AM here, right now). Real examples from El Viaje del Jaguar.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Split screen: a permanent mountain vs a passing cloud. Text: 'SER vs ESTAR'
[5-15s] HOST: 'Two verbs for TO BE. This is where most Spanish courses lose you. But Yaguara makes it simple. SER is for what things ARE — their essence. ESTAR is for where things are or how they feel right now.'
[15-25s] HOST: 'From the game — SER: El rio es frio — The river IS cold. That's its nature. La ceiba es alta — The ceiba tree IS tall. That's permanent. La rana es pequena y dorada — The frog IS small and golden. That's what it IS.'
[25-35s] HOST: 'ESTAR: Yo estoy aqui — I AM here. Right now. Tu estas aqui — You ARE here. Temporary. Estoy feliz — I'm happy (right now). Estoy en la selva — I'm in the jungle (location).'
[35-48s] HOST: 'Yaguara in Destination 3 says: El mundo es grande. Es verde y bonito — The world IS big. It IS green and beautiful. That's SER — describing what the world IS. But then: Tu y yo estamos aqui — You and I ARE here. That's ESTAR — we're here now, in this moment.'
[48-60s] HOST: 'SER = essence. ESTAR = state. The mountain IS tall, SER. The cloud IS here, ESTAR. Practice with 15,000 games at babelfree.com'

youtube.title: How to Describe Things in Spanish — Grande, Pequeno, Bonito
youtube.description: Learn 6 essential adjectives: grande, pequeno, bonito, fuerte, feliz, bueno. In the game, Yaguara asks 'Como es el mundo?' — and now you can answer. Free at babelfree.com.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Yaguara's voice: 'Como es el mundo?' Text fades in with each adjective.
[5-12s] HOST: 'Destination 3 is all about describing the world. Yaguara asks: Como es? — What is it like? Here are 6 words to answer.'
[12-20s] Giant ceiba tree. 'Grande — big, large. Gran-de. La ceiba es grande — the ceiba tree is big.'
[20-27s] Tiny golden frog. 'Pequeno — small. Pe-KE-nyo. La rana es pequena — the frog is small. Notice: pequenO for masculine, pequenA for feminine.'
[27-34s] Sunset. 'Bonito — beautiful, pretty. Bo-NEE-to. El mundo es bonito — the world is beautiful.'
[34-40s] Jaguar stance. 'Fuerte — strong. FWER-te. El jaguar es fuerte — the jaguar is strong. This one doesn't change for gender.'
[40-46s] Smiling face. 'Feliz — happy. Fe-LEEZ. Estoy feliz — I'm happy. Also doesn't change.'
[46-52s] Thumbs up. 'Bueno — good. BWE-no. El dia es bueno — the day is good.'
[52-60s] HOST: 'In the game, when you describe something, it glows brighter. Names give things life. Descriptions give them color. babelfree.com'

youtube.title: Learn Spanish Through Colombian Literature — Meet Rafael Pombo
youtube.description: In El Viaje del Jaguar, you don't just learn vocabulary — you enter the worlds of real Colombian authors. Meet Rafael Pombo, Colombia's greatest children's poet, and his character Rinrin Renacuajo. Literary Spanish learning at babelfree.com.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Old book opens. A frog jumps out. Text: 'Meet the poet.'
[5-18s] HOST: 'In Destination 3 of El Viaje del Jaguar, a figure appears between the trees — a man with a hat and an old book. This is Rafael Pombo, Colombia's greatest children's poet. And from his book jumps Rinrin — a tadpole who becomes your companion.'
[18-30s] HOST: 'Pombo wrote Rinrin Renacuajo in 1883 — it's basically the Colombian equivalent of a Mother Goose rhyme. In the game, Rinrin jumps out and says: Hola! Yo soy Rinrin! And just like that, literature becomes your Spanish teacher.'
[30-42s] HOST: 'This is what makes Babelfree different. You're not memorizing flashcards. You're walking through the fictional worlds of four Colombian authors — Pombo, Rivera, Carrasquilla, and Macias — and learning Spanish the way stories teach language: through immersion.'
[42-52s] HOST: 'Yaguara, the jaguar, literally enters and exits these authors' worlds as a game mechanic. One moment you're in the rainforest, the next you're inside a 19th-century Colombian poem.'
[52-60s] HOST: 'Literature + games + Colombian ecosystems = Babelfree. 89 destinations, completely free. babelfree.com'

youtube.title: The Nature Words That'll Transform Your Spanish
youtube.description: Rio, arbol, flor, agua, selva, sol, luna — learn the nature vocabulary of the Colombian rainforest. These words from Destination 2 of El Viaje del Jaguar will make you see the natural world in Spanish.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Drone shot of Colombian jungle. Text: 'The world has names. Learn them.'
[5-12s] HOST: 'In the game, Yaguara says: Cada cosa tiene un nombre. Cuando dices el nombre, la cosa vive — Every thing has a name. When you say the name, the thing lives. Let's bring nature to life.'
[12-20s] River. 'El rio — the river. REE-oh. In Colombia, rivers are sacred. The game starts at the Rio Atrato.'
[20-27s] Tree. 'El arbol — the tree. AR-bol. And the greatest tree: la ceiba — the ceiba, the tree of life in Colombian mythology.'
[27-33s] Flower. 'La flor — the flower. Flor. Short and sweet. La flor espera su nombre — the flower waits for its name.'
[33-39s] Water. 'El agua — water. AH-gwa. Careful — it's EL agua even though it ends in A.'
[39-45s] Jungle. 'La selva — the jungle, rainforest. SEL-va. Colombia has the largest rainforest per capita in the world.'
[45-52s] Sun and moon. 'El sol — the sun. La luna — the moon. Yaguara counts lunas — moons — to track time.'
[52-60s] HOST: 'Seven nature words. You just named the world. Nombrar es crear. babelfree.com'

youtube.title: Food Words in Spanish — Comida, Fruta, Pescado, Agua
youtube.description: Learn essential food vocabulary in Spanish from Destination 5, where the baby jaguar Rio catches a fish and screams 'Me gusta el pescado!' Comida, fruta, pescado, agua — the basics of eating and drinking.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Splash! A fish jumps. Text: 'Me gusta el pescado!'
[5-15s] HOST: 'In Destination 5, a baby jaguar named Rio bursts out of the river with a fish in his mouth screaming ME GUSTA EL PESCADO — I like fish! Let's learn from his appetite.'
[15-23s] 'La comida — food. Co-MEE-da. This is the big umbrella word for all food.'
[23-30s] 'La fruta — fruit. FROO-ta. Colombia has fruits you've never heard of — guanabana, lulo, maracuya.'
[30-37s] 'El pescado — fish (as food). Pes-CA-do. Remember: el pez is a living fish, el pescado is fish you eat.'
[37-43s] 'El agua — water. AH-gwa. Bebo agua — I drink water. That's one of the first things Yaguara does every day.'
[43-50s] 'Comer — to eat. CO-mer. Beber — to drink. Be-BER. Two essential verbs.'
[50-60s] HOST: 'In the game, Rio says: Me gusta el agua! Me gusta el pescado! Me gusta TODO! Be like Rio — love everything, eat everything, learn everything. babelfree.com'

youtube.title: Say Hello and Goodbye in Spanish — Beyond Just 'Hola'
youtube.description: Master greetings and farewells with real dialogue from El Viaje del Jaguar: Hola, Adios, Buenos dias, and the essential question Quien eres tu? From Destination 1 of Babelfree.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Golden jaguar eyes open. A voice: 'Hola.' Pause. 'Yo soy Yaguara.'
[5-15s] HOST: 'The very first word you hear in El Viaje del Jaguar is Hola. One word. Two syllables. The beginning of everything. But there's more to greetings than just Hola.'
[15-25s] 'Hola — hello. The universal greeting. OH-la. In Colombia, it's warm, soft, sometimes just a breath.'
[25-32s] 'Adios — goodbye. Ah-dee-OS. In the game, Rinrin the tadpole says: Adios, Yaguara! — as he bounces away.'
[32-40s] 'Quien eres tu? — Who are you? Key-EN EH-res TOO? This is the closing question of Destination 1. Yaguara asks it after she introduces herself.'
[40-48s] 'Yo soy ___ — I am ___. The answer. Yo soy Yaguara. Yo soy Rinrin. Yo soy... you.'
[48-55s] HOST: 'In the game, when you say your name, it glows in the air como una luciernaga — like a firefly. Your name is the first light you create.'
[55-60s] HOST: 'Nombrar es crear. To name is to create. Start naming at babelfree.com'

youtube.title: What Are Colombia's 8 Ecosystems? Learn Spanish Geography
youtube.description: Bosque, costa, desierto, islas, llanos, nevada, selva, sierra — the 8 Colombian ecosystems that form the game world of El Viaje del Jaguar. Learn geography AND Spanish at the same time.
youtube.script:
[0-5s] Map of Colombia with 8 colored regions. Text: '8 ecosystems. 8 worlds. 89 destinations.'
[5-12s] HOST: 'El Viaje del Jaguar takes you through all 8 ecosystems of Colombia. Each one teaches different vocabulary, different sounds, different stories.'
[12-18s] Forest. 'El bosque — the forest. BOS-ke. Dense, green, alive.'
[18-24s] Coast. 'La costa — the coast. COS-ta. Caribbean beaches, coral reefs, pelicans.'
[24-30s] Desert. 'El desierto — the desert. De-see-ER-to. Think La Guajira — sand dunes under ancient skies.'
[30-35s] Islands. 'Las islas — the islands. EES-las. San Andres, Providencia — turquoise water.'
[35-40s] Plains. 'Los llanos — the plains. YAH-nos. Cowboys, endless grasslands, sunsets.'
[40-45s] Snow. 'La nevada — the snow-capped mountains. Ne-VA-da. Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta.'
[45-50s] Jungle. 'La selva — the deep jungle. SEL-va. Amazon territory.'
[50-55s] Mountains. 'La sierra — the mountain range. See-EH-rra. The Andes.'
[55-60s] HOST: 'Every ecosystem has its own vocabulary, its own animals, its own stories. 89 destinations, 18,152 games. All free. babelfree.com'

youtube.title: The Philosophy Behind Babelfree — Nombrar Es Crear
youtube.description: Why does naming things in Spanish make them glow? The Colombian philosophy behind El Viaje del Jaguar: 'Nombrar es crear' — to name is to create. How literary immersion teaches language better than flashcards.
youtube.script:
[0-8s] Darkness. A voice whispers: 'Nombrar es crear.' One by one, words appear and glow: rio, selva, arbol, rana, jaguar.
[8-20s] HOST: 'Most language apps treat words like data. Memorize, repeat, forget. But El Viaje del Jaguar is built on a different idea — one from Colombian author Luis Fernando Macias: Nombrar es crear — to name is to create. When you learn a word, you don't just add it to a list. You bring something into existence.'
[20-32s] HOST: 'In the game, when Yaguara names the river — el rio — it glows. When she names the tree — el arbol — it lights up. Things without names literally fade. This isn't just a game mechanic. This is how language actually works. When you learn a new word, you can suddenly SEE something you couldn't see before.'
[32-45s] HOST: 'Babelfree teaches through four Colombian authors: Pombo, Rivera, Carrasquilla, and Macias. Their stories become your textbook. Their characters become your companions. Rinrin the tadpole. The jaguar Yaguara. The wise ceiba tree.'
[45-55s] HOST: 'From A1 to C2, 89 destinations, 8 ecosystems, a dictionary in 94 languages with 11.8 million words. And it's completely free. Because naming things should be free.'
[55-60s] Text: 'Nombrar es crear. babelfree.com'

instagram.caption:
Your first word in Spanish: HOLA.

In El Viaje del Jaguar, this is how it all begins. A jaguar opens her golden eyes in the darkness and says one word: Hola.

Hola means hello. But in this game, it means something more — it means you've arrived.

Yaguara says: 'Yo soy Yaguara. Y tu eres... viajero.'
You are a traveler. And the journey starts now.

Free at babelfree.com
instagram.visualSuggestion: 15-second reel: dark screen, golden jaguar eyes open slowly, text 'Hola' fades in with warm gold glow, then 'Yo soy Yaguara' appears letter by letter. End with babelfree.com logo.

instagram.caption:
WORD OF THE DAY: La rana (the frog)

Pronunciation: RAH-nah
Gender: Feminine (LA rana)

From the game: 'La rana es pequena y dorada' — The frog is small and golden.

In Colombia, the golden poison frog is one of the most iconic animals of the Choco rainforest. In El Viaje del Jaguar, the golden frog's silence is the first sign that something is wrong in the world.

Save this for your vocabulary journal.
babelfree.com
instagram.visualSuggestion: Carousel: Slide 1 — 'La rana' in large text over photo of golden poison frog. Slide 2 — pronunciation guide with phonetic breakdown. Slide 3 — example sentence from the game. Slide 4 — cultural note about Colombian golden frogs. Slide 5 — 'Save this word' CTA with babelfree.com.

instagram.caption:
6 colors in Spanish you need to know:

1. Verde — green (like the selva)
2. Rojo — red (like the sunset over the llanos)
3. Azul — blue (like the Caribbean coast)
4. Blanco — white (like the Sierra Nevada)
5. Negro — black (like the night in the bosque)
6. Amarillo — yellow (like the sun over the islas)

In the game, Yaguara asks: 'Como es el mundo?' — What is the world like?

Now you can answer with color.
babelfree.com
instagram.visualSuggestion: Carousel: 6 slides, each a Colombian landscape dominated by one color. Slide 1 — green jungle + 'verde.' Slide 2 — red sunset + 'rojo.' Slide 3 — blue ocean + 'azul.' Slide 4 — white snow + 'blanco.' Slide 5 — dark forest + 'negro.' Slide 6 — yellow sun + 'amarillo.' Final slide: the question 'Como es tu mundo?'

instagram.caption:
NOMBRAR ES CREAR.
To name is to create.

This is the philosophy behind El Viaje del Jaguar. When you learn a word in Spanish, you don't memorize it — you bring something to life.

In the game, things without names literally fade into darkness. The river. The tree. The frog. They wait for you to name them.

'Cada cosa tiene un nombre. Y cada nombre tiene una luz.'
— Every thing has a name. And every name has a light.

Start naming the world. babelfree.com
instagram.visualSuggestion: Reel: slow cinematic sequence. Dark screen. Words appear one by one and glow: 'el rio' (river appears), 'la selva' (jungle appears), 'el arbol' (tree appears), 'la rana' (frog appears). Then text: 'Nombrar es crear.' Warm golden tone throughout.

instagram.caption:
Meet YAGUARA.

She's a female jaguar. She walks between worlds. She's your spirit guide through 89 destinations across Colombia.

Her first words to you:
'Hola. Yo soy Yaguara. El jaguar que camina entre los mundos.'

(Hello. I am Yaguara. The jaguar who walks between worlds.)

She doesn't care where you're going. She cares that you opened your eyes.

'Abre los ojos.' — Open your eyes.

babelfree.com

Slide 4 — 'She is your guide through 89 destinations.' Slide 5 — 'Abre los ojos. Open your eyes. babelfree.com'

instagram.caption:
How Yaguara counts time:

'Una luna. Dos lunas. Tres lunas.'
One moon. Two moons. Three moons.

In El Viaje del Jaguar, there are no clocks. Yaguara counts moons reflected in the river.

Uno (1) Dos (2) Tres (3) Cuatro (4) Cinco (5)
Seis (6) Siete (7) Ocho (8) Nueve (9) Diez (10)

Swipe to count with her.

instagram.caption:
Daily routine in Spanish, Yaguara-style:

Yo despierto con el sol. (I wake up with the sun.)
Camino al rio. (I walk to the river.)
Bebo agua. (I drink water.)
Como fruta. (I eat fruit.)
Escucho los sonidos. (I listen to the sounds.)
Descanso en un arbol. (I rest in a tree.)

Notice the pattern? For YO (I), verbs end in -O.
DespiertO. CaminO. BebO. ComO. EscuchO. DescansO.

That's present tense. You just learned it.
babelfree.com

instagram.caption:
EL vs LA: A quick cheat sheet

EL (masculine):
el rio — the river
el arbol — the tree
el pajaro — the bird
el sol — the sun
el pez — the fish

LA (feminine):
la selva — the jungle
la rana — the frog
la flor — the flower
la luna — the moon
la fruta — the fruit

General rule: -O endings = EL, -A endings = LA
Exception: el agua, el dia

Save this. You'll need it.
babelfree.com
instagram.visualSuggestion: Carousel: Slide 1 — 'EL vs LA' in large bold text, split-color background (blue/gold). Slides 2-3 — EL words with icons. Slides 4-5 — LA words with icons. Slide 6 — exceptions. Slide 7 — 'Save this cheat sheet. babelfree.com'

instagram.caption:
SPOT THE PATTERN:

Yo soy Yaguara. (I am Yaguara.)
Tu eres viajero. (You are a traveler.)
El es poeta. (He is a poet.)

Soy, eres, es — three forms of SER (to be).

Yo SOY — I am
Tu ERES — you are
El/Ella ES — he/she is

You just learned the most important verb in Spanish.

babelfree.com

instagram.caption:
How to say what you like in Spanish:

Me gusta + singular noun
'Me gusta el agua.' — I like water.

Me gustan + plural noun
'Me gustan los sonidos.' — I like the sounds.

No me gusta + noun
'No me gusta el silencio.' — I don't like silence.

The secret: the verb agrees with WHAT YOU LIKE, not with YOU.

That's why Rio says: 'Me gustan los peces' — not 'Me gusto los peces.'

babelfree.com

instagram.caption:
Yaguara's morning routine (and yours, in Spanish):

Primero, yo despierto. (First, I wake up.)
Despues, camino al rio. (Then, I walk to the river.)
Luego, descanso. (Later, I rest.)

Three time words:
Primero = first
Despues = then/after
Luego = later

With just these three words, you can describe any sequence in Spanish.

What's YOUR primero?

tiktok.script:
[0-3s] TEXT: 'Spanish words that sound like what they mean.'
[3-7s] HOST: 'Chapuzon — a big splash. CHAPOOTHON. Sounds splashy, right?'
[7-11s] 'Susurro — a whisper. Soo-SOO-rro. Sounds like whispering.'
[11-15s] 'Trueno — thunder. TROO-eh-no. Sounds like it rumbles.'
[15-19s] 'Resplandor — a glow. Res-plan-DOR. Sounds golden.'
[19-23s] 'In the game, when Rio the baby jaguar jumps in the river: SPLASH — un gran chapuzon.'
[23-28s] TEXT: 'Spanish sounds like the world. babelfree.com'
tiktok.hook: Spanish words that sound like what they mean

linkedin.content:
Babelfree is a free, literary Spanish language learning platform built on a single Colombian philosophy: nombrar es crear — to name is to create.

Our flagship program, El Viaje del Jaguar (The Jaguar's Journey), takes learners from A1 to C2 through 89 destinations across 8 Colombian ecosystems — bosque, costa, desierto, islas, llanos, nevada, selva, and sierra — guided by Yaguara, a female jaguar spirit who walks between worlds.

What makes us different:

- Literary immersion, not flashcards. Learners enter the fictional worlds of four Colombian authors — Rafael Pombo, Jose Eustasio Rivera, Tomas Carrasquilla, and Luis Fernando Macias — and acquire language through narrative, not memorization.
- 18,152 games across 89 destinations, following a 7-stage word acquisition pipeline grounded in Bruner's spiral curriculum, Steiner's imagination-thinking-doing progression, and resonant repetition.
- A free multilingual dictionary spanning 94 languages with 11.8 million words and 17.5 million definitions.
- 100% free. No ads. No subscriptions. No paywalls.

Our CEFR-aligned curriculum prepares learners for DELE certification (Instituto Cervantes) while going beyond conventional frameworks. Immersion is level-gated: A1-A2 Basic content uses the learner's native language; A2 Advanced through C2 is conducted entirely in Spanish.

Built with love in Colombia. Open to the world.

https://babelfree.com

We just launched Babelfree — and it's completely free.

El Viaje del Jaguar is a Spanish language learning platform unlike anything that exists today. Here's why:

Most language apps treat words like data points. Memorize. Repeat. Forget. We built something different: a literary game where you travel through 89 destinations across Colombia's 8 ecosystems, guided by a jaguar named Yaguara, learning Spanish through the works of four Colombian authors.

18,152 games. 8,500+ words. A1 to C2. No ads. No subscriptions.

The philosophy behind it comes from Colombian author Luis Fernando Macias: "Nombrar es crear" — to name is to create. In the game, when you learn a Spanish word, the thing you name literally comes to life. Things without names fade into darkness.

We also built a free multilingual dictionary: 94 languages, 11.8 million words, 17.5 million definitions.

Why free? Because we believe language access shouldn't have a paywall. The ability to name the world — in any language — should belong to everyone.

https://babelfree.com

#LanguageLearning #EdTech #LearnSpanish #Colombia #FreeEducation #Babelfree

What if your Spanish textbook was a jaguar?

At Babelfree, we replaced the traditional language learning model with something radical: literary immersion through Colombian storytelling.

Instead of vocabulary lists, learners name the natural world alongside Yaguara, a female jaguar spirit guide. Instead of grammar drills, they enter the fictional worlds of Rafael Pombo (Colombia's greatest children's poet, 1883), Jose Eustasio Rivera, Tomas Carrasquilla, and Luis Fernando Macias.

The pedagogical model draws from:
- Bruner's spiral curriculum (concepts return at deeper levels)
- Steiner's imagination-thinking-doing progression
- A 7-stage word acquisition pipeline: passive exposure, recognition, matching, guided production, free production, contextual transfer, spiral return

Every word passes through all 7 stages. Every structure is introduced before it's tested. Zero surprises at the summit.

This isn't gamification as decoration. The game IS the curriculum.

89 destinations. 8 ecosystems. 18,152 games. Completely free.

https://babelfree.com

#EdTech #LanguageAcquisition #CurriculumDesign #SpanishLearning #Colombia #Pedagogy

We built a free dictionary for 94 languages. Here's what we learned.

Babelfree's multilingual dictionary contains 11.8 million words and 17.5 million definitions across 94 languages — from Spanish and French to Swahili and Welsh.

But we made a deliberate design choice: these are native-first dictionaries. Definitions are written in the base language, not translated from English. A Spanish word is defined in Spanish. A Japanese word is defined in Japanese.

Cross-language connections exist as hyperlinked equivalents — not "translations." Because translation implies a 1:1 mapping that rarely exists between languages. "Saudade" is not "nostalgia." "Hygge" is not "coziness." Languages deserve their own definitions.

The top languages by coverage: French (1.7M words), English (1M), German (1M), Latin (852K), Greek (768K), Spanish (687K), Dutch (672K), Russian (474K), Italian (416K), Portuguese (403K).

All CEFR-leveled. All free. All at babelfree.com/dictionary.

#Linguistics #Dictionary #Multilingual #LanguageLearning #NLP #FreeTools #Babelfree

"Nombrar es crear" — To name is to create.

This phrase from Colombian author Luis Fernando Macias is the foundation of everything we built at Babelfree.

In cognitive linguistics, there's a well-documented phenomenon: learning a word for something changes your ability to perceive it. Russian speakers who have separate words for light blue (goluboy) and dark blue (siniy) literally perceive those colors faster than English speakers.

We built an entire Spanish learning platform around this idea. In El Viaje del Jaguar, things without names fade into darkness. When you learn the word "rio" (river), the river appears. When you learn "selva" (jungle), the canopy glows.

It's not just a game mechanic. It's how language acquisition actually works. Every new word doesn't just add to your vocabulary — it adds to your reality.

89 destinations. 18,152 games. A1 to C2. Free forever.

https://babelfree.com

#LanguageAcquisition #CognitiveLinguistics #SpanishLearning #EdTech #Colombia #NombrarEsCrear

From A1 to C2, here's how El Viaje del Jaguar structures the journey:

Destination 1: "Abre los ojos" (Open your eyes) — You learn hola, yo soy, tu eres. Identity.
Destination 2: "Los nombres de las cosas" (The names of things) — rio, arbol, rana, selva. The natural world.
Destination 3: "Como es el mundo?" (What is the world like?) — grande, pequeno, verde, azul. Description.
Destination 4: "Contar los dias" (Counting the days) — uno through diez, days of the week. Time.
Destination 5: "Lo que me gusta" (What I like) — me gusta, no me gusta. Preference.
Destination 6: "Un dia en el mundo" (A day in the world) — caminar, nadar, comer, beber. Daily routine.

Notice the progression: identity, naming, describing, quantifying, expressing preference, narrating. This mirrors the natural order of first language acquisition.

By destination 58, learners are reading Colombian literary texts at C2 level — the same authors whose characters accompanied them from the very first "Hola."

That's the journey. That's the jaguar.

https://babelfree.com

#CurriculumDesign #CEFR #SpanishLearning #LanguageAcquisition #EdTech #Babelfree
